title: Supporting Children on Therapy Waitlists: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Web-Based Parent-Focused Single Session Intervention for Child Anxiety
authors: Madelaine Abel, Jessica L. Schleider, Jenna Sung, Alayna D'Amico, Dina R. Hirshfeld-Becker
description: Many children experience significant, impairing anxiety, yet most never access evidence-based support; among those who do, most face long wait-times to be seen. This disconnect reflects a need for intervention approaches that provide rapid access to interim care. Single-session interventions (SSIs) may help mitigate the adverse impacts of long waitlists. This pilot parallel group RCT evaluated the effectiveness and acceptability of a self-guided, web-based SSI delivered to parents of children ages 4-13 (Mage=8.84) with anxiety or OCD on a waitlist at a hospital-based Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy (CBT) clinic. The SSI, Project EMPOWER, targets parental accommodation, a family factor that can worsen child anxiety and impede efficacy of CBT. Parents were randomized to receive the SSI (N=24) or waitlist as usual (N=23). Parents completed a baseline diagnostic interview about their child and self-report questionnaires about their accommodation of child anxiety, child anxiety symptoms, and perceived ability to manage their child’s anxiety (parent agency) at baseline, 2-week follow-up, and CBT intake. Relative to controls, parents who received the SSI reported significant reductions in their accommodation (d=0.59, p=.046) and child anxiety (d=1.25, p<.001) and significant increases in parent agency (d=0.68, p=.02) from baseline to 2-week follow-up. Effects were maintained until children started CBT (mean time =13.54 weeks). Additionally, parents rated the SSI as highly acceptable in accordance with preregistered benchmarks. Results support the effectiveness and acceptability of a web-based, self-guided SSI for parents of children on therapy waitlists, offering a scalable approach to support families while they wait for formal treatment.

title: Targeting coping strategies, emotion regulation, and self-esteem in children with dyslexia to support mental health: A randomised-controlled trial of Clever Kids
authors: Mark Boyes, Kirsten Melville, Mandy Nayton, Hannah Pizzey, Suze Leitao
description: Background: Children with dyslexia are at elevated risk of mental health difficulties, both internalising (emotional) and externalising (behavioural) problems. Clever Kids is a nine-week socio-emotional wellbeing programme developed specifically to support upper primary school children with dyslexia. Aims: We conducted a pre-registered randomised-controlled trial to test the efficacy of Clever Kids. Method: Seventy-eight children (Mage = 10.19 years, 54% female) with clinically diagnosed dyslexia took part in the trial. Children were randomised to either attend Clever Kids (n = 38) or to a wait-list control condition (n = 40). Coping skills, emotion regulation, self-esteem, perseverance, assertiveness, as well as mental health (internalising and externalising symptoms) were measured at pre-programme, post-programme, and at three-month follow-up using well-validated measures. Results: The predicted interactions between intervention condition and time were significant for six outcomes. Attending Clever Kids was associated with significant improvements in child-reported coping, emotion regulation, academic and social self-esteem, as well as parent-reported anxiety and depression. Effects were all maintained at three-month follow-up. Unexpectedly, being assigned to the wait-list condition was associated with improvements in perseverance at follow-up assessment. For all other outcomes, interactions between intervention condition and time were non-significant. Conclusion: Clever Kids appears a promising programme for supporting socio-emotional wellbeing of children with dyslexia. Future research should consider incorporating caregivers more explicitly into the programme and alternative delivery modes, which could expand the reach of the programme.

title: A randomized controlled trial exploring dose-response effects of mindfulness meditation practice on wellbeing
authors: Nicholas I. Bowles, Nicholas T Van Dam, Jonathan N Davies, Julieta Galante
description:
Background/Objectives: Psychological benefits have been demonstrated across a variety of different meditation offerings. There remains, however, limited evidence clarifying how different ‘doses’ of meditation relate to benefits, highlighting fundamental limitations for clinical best practice. The present study aimed to examine dose–response effects across a range of daily mindfulness meditation durations in a well-powered randomized controlled experiment, while also investigating whether actual practice behaviour predicts improved outcomes, independent of assigned dose.
Method: A total of 716 meditation-naïve, healthy adults were randomized to one of four daily mindfulness practice conditions (4-, 10-, 20-, or 30-minutes) over a 28-day online intervention. The primary outcome was wellbeing, and secondary outcomes included mindfulness-related constructs and mental health symptoms. Primary analyses used a constrained longitudinal design within an intention-to-treat approach to test for differences between assigned dose groups. Secondary per-protocol analyses were conducted to reduce effects of nonadherence. Exploratory analyses examined five measures of practice dose, alongside motivation and expectancy variables, as predictors of outcomes.
Results: Significant improvements were observed across all outcomes and arms from baseline to post-intervention (|SMD| = 0.29–0.56, FDR-adjusted p < .05). We found no evidence of a dose–response effect based on assigned practice dose in either intention-to-treat or per-protocol analyses for any outcome (all p’s > .05). While 70.9% of those in the 10-minute group completed 2/3 of sessions, only 65.3% and 48.5% of participants did the same in the 20- and 30-minute groups, respectively. In exploratory analyses, practice engagement was significantly related to 8/10 outcomes ([β] = 0.05-0.14), while goal confidence was significantly related to 9/10 outcomes ([β] = 0.08-0.21), and time was significantly related to changes on all outcomes ([β] = 0.26-0.54; FDR-adjusted p < .05). 
Conclusions: These findings suggest that within the first 30 days of practice among non-clinical, beginner populations there is no evidence that longer doses (up to 30 min) are more beneficial than shorter doses (down to 5 min) of daily meditation, while practice engagement and confidence in the efficacy of meditation may impact effects.

title: A re-analysis of Chiang et al. (2017): Cognitive-behaviour therapy does not have a statistically significant effect on levels of depression or mania in bipolar disorder
authors: David Smailes, Milo Johns, Peter Moseley
description: There is an ongoing debate concerning the efficacy of cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT) for bipolar disorder. Chiang et al.’s (2017) meta-analysis concluded that CBT was effective in reducing symptoms of depression and mania severity in patients with bipolar disorder, and that article has been cited more than 200 times. However, Chiang et al.’s conclusions are based, in part, on a pair of implausibly large effect sizes that were extracted from a randomized controlled trial published in 2010. In this paper, we repeat Chiang et al.’s analyses, but replace the pair of implausibly large effect sizes with much more plausible effect sizes taken from a second journal article that reported results of that 2010 randomized controlled trial. Our findings suggest that Chiang et al. should have reported that CBT does not have a statistically significant effect on symptoms of depression and mania severity in patients with bipolar disorder. Data supporting our findings are available at this – doi.org/10.17605/osf.io/x3qwp – page on the Open Science Framework.

title: Gaming Disorder:  A qualitative meta-synthesis of related case studies
authors: Tiina Auranen, Matus Adamkovic, Marcel Martončik, Solip Park, Joona Ville-Valtteri Kauraoja, Yaewon Jin, Ville Malinen, Mikhail Fiadotau, Sabina Hnatová, Anna Urbancová, Lenka Vargová, Jana Papcunová, Artem Buts, Sunny Choi, Veli-Matti Karhulahti
description: Several meta-analyses and reviews have been published on gaming-related health problems, mainly in the context of addictive behaviours and gaming disorder in particular. However, none of those reviews have been qualitative or focused on individual cases. The present meta-synthesis provides such evidence from 88 published case studies with 133 individual cases. Surprisingly, almost no case study evidence was found from high-prevalence countries like China and South Korea. Using deductive coding, the study identifies and synthesises a total of 845 problems and 35 comorbidities along with reasons for seeking help—most of treatment-seeking continues to happen due to social compliance. Further, we applied thematic synthesis to qualitatively organise the data into a network, represented by connected areas where a spectrum of vulnerabilities sets the individual on a high-stress life path; self-medication through gaming becomes both the cure and the problem; and prolonged gaming or critical life events lead to treatment or even hospitalization. After sensitivity analyses, primary findings generally hold throughout levels of informativeness across studies. We find many of the reported problems and their contexts aligning with coping theories of gaming disorder. Next to the above thematic areas, which characterises a large part of the published studies, we highlight the great variety of content across cases: most studies did not address, diagnose, or mention gaming disorder formally at all, but rather represented instances of life where gaming was related to health problems in other ways.

title: Indigenous Perspectives of Trauma-Sensitive Yoga Using Community-Based and Consensual Qualitative Frameworks
authors: Kelsey Madison Dietrich, Linda Zheng, Ashley Baumann, Jill Kessler, Lyndsey Ducheneaux, Viann N Nguyen-Feng
description:
Objective: Indigenous communities endure intergenerational and ongoing traumas. Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga (TCTSY) is an empirically-supported yoga-based intervention for complex trauma. To become a TCTSY-Facilitator, candidates complete a 20-hour training followed by a 300-hour certification program. This community-based research documented the first Indigenous TCTSY-Facilitator Training Cohort’s perspectives on TCTSY’s acceptability, feasibility, and utility. 
Method: Protocols were developed with community partners, and researchers engaged in reflexivity practices and Indigenous cultural learnings. Twenty interviews were conducted with 15 adult interviewees: 14 program participants (13 women) who completed the 20-hour training and 1 program mentor. From the 14 program participants (11 Great Plains Tribal Nations, with 4 Tribal citizens and 7 descendents), 10 enrolled in the 300-hour training, and 3 completed the full certification. Interviews were analyzed using the consensual qualitative research method. 
Results: Most participants reported the training positively impacted their approach to trauma care and some aspects (e.g., body-based modality) aligned with current healing approaches. Common training takeaways included learning about the effects of trauma and the value of invitational language. Participants endorsed intentions to integrate TCTSY for personal and community healing; examples of integration included smudging/burning of medicines (e.g., sage), land-based practice for connection to nature, and intentional movement within ceremony space. Program improvements included additional emphasis on yoga history/philosophy, collaborations with Elders, and acknowledgment of current community trauma. 
Conclusion: A community-based research framework provided opportunities to explore the feasibility, acceptability, and utility of TCTSY for Indigenous communities in a culturally-relevant and trauma-informed way.

title: Too Little, Too Much: Evidence for Quadratic Patterns in Emotion Regulation Strategy Use in Daily Life
authors: Dominique Maciejewski, Yasemin Erbas, Tak Tsun Lo, Egon Dejonckheere, Merlijn Olthof, Andrea Bunge, Eeske van Roekel
description: Emotion characteristics, like emotion intensity and perceived controllability, shape how individuals regulate their emotions. While the link between emotion characteristics and emotion regulation strategies seems clear for some strategies, findings are mixed for others. This study proposes that individuals are most likely to engage in emotion regulation at average levels of emotion intensity and controllability, and less likely at low or high levels (i.e., when emotions are either too weak to require regulation or too overwhelming to regulate). To test this hypothesis, we used data from a 60-day diary study in 79 adults (mean age = 22.29, SD = 6.08, 84% female), who every evening reported on emotion characteristics (emotion intensity and controllability) and strategy use (distraction, rumination, reappraisal, expression, suppression, relaxation). In addition to single strategies, we also examined emotion regulation strategy switching (i.e., the extent to which individuals varied their strategy composition). We replicate some linear findings, including that expression, relaxation, and rumination were used on days with more intense and uncontrollable emotions (for expression only). Importantly, we provide novel evidence for quadratic relations, where suppression and reappraisal were used on days where emotions were of average intensity and controllability, and less on days where emotions were less or more intense and controllable than usual. Moreover, individuals were more likely to switch to strategies they typically do not use on days where emotions were less or more controllable than usual. These findings highlight the value of moving beyond linear relations when studying emotion regulation dynamics in daily life.
